species: Snowmelt Flood Surge -- high-energy spring flood, river canyon, Pacific Northwest, annual hydrological maximum
scientific_name: Abiotic hydrological event -- Pacific Northwest mountain river at annual snowmelt peak discharge. No taxonomic classification. Primary rivers: Skagit, White, Methow, Hoh (Washington State).
sound_type: High-energy spring snowmelt flood surge. Acoustic layers: (1) Broadband turbulence noise (20-8,000+ Hz, continuous, 65-80 dB at 30m at peak discharge); (2) Bedload transport drumming (10-80 Hz, boulder and rock rolling impacts, irregular continuous during peak transport -- most distinctive maximum-discharge acoustic signature, absent at low/moderate flow); (3) Standing wave oscillation (0.5-3 Hz pulse at canyon constrictions, at annual amplitude peak during snowmelt maximum, physically felt at canyon proximity); (4) Spray and mist high-frequency component (2,000-8,000 Hz, more prominent at maximum kinetic energy). Canyon reverb: 1-3 second reverb time in narrow rocky canyon, creates layered overlapping acoustic environment. Peak discharge 3-10x annual average. E=7 through sustained enveloping broadband complexity across all frequencies simultaneously.
ecosystem: River canyon, Pacific Northwest mountain river systems. Elevation-dependent peak timing: lowland rivers March-April, mid-elevation April-May, high-elevation May-June. Snowpack source: Pacific Northwest Cascades 10-30 feet at 4,000+ ft elevation in high snowpack years. Canyon amplification: rocky narrow canyon walls create reverberant environment, amplifying river acoustic output relative to open valley terrain. Climate change: snowpack declining, pulse shifting earlier, becoming compressed, summer flows decreasing.
season: Spring, April-June. Elevation-dependent peak: high-snowpack years, high-elevation rivers (White, upper Skagit, Hoh glacial component) peak May-June. Lower-elevation rivers peak April-May. Annual event -- predictable timing, variable magnitude based on winter snowpack accumulation.
family: Hydrology (abiotic). No taxonomic classification. River system context: Skagit River (largest WA State by volume, North Cascades drainage), White River (Mount Rainier southeast flank), Methow River (eastern Cascades Okanogan County continental snowmelt), Hoh River (Olympic Mountains Olympic NP, combined snowmelt + glacial, surge sustained through July high snowpack years).
milestone: ANNUAL HYDROLOGICAL MAXIMUM OF THE PACIFIC NORTHWEST RIVER SYSTEM. Bedload transport drumming (10-80 Hz) is the most distinctive acoustic signature -- present only at maximum discharge, absent at low/moderate flow. This is a sound that only occurs when the river is at its annual maximum: not just a big river, but the river at the specific condition of annual peak. Canyon reverb (1-3 sec) creates acoustic layering not present in open terrain. Salmon smolt outmigration timed to this peak flow: the sound is concurrent with one of the Pacific Northwest ecosystem's most critical annual biological events.

coordinates.description: Representative: Upper Skagit River canyon, Skagit County WA, between Concrete and Marblemount. 48.44°N 121.55°W. Largest WA river by volume; North Cascades snowmelt; most powerful spring surge in state. Also: White River canyon William O. Douglas Wilderness (46.93°N 121.56°W); Hoh River valley Olympic NP (47.86°N 123.93°W); Methow River valley Okanogan County.

acoustic_data.broadband_foundation: Turbulent water at high velocity (3-10x annual average discharge). Continuous broadband noise 20-8,000+ Hz. Amplitude 65-80 dB at 30m. The acoustic foundation against which more specific sounds emerge.
acoustic_data.bedload_transport: Rock and boulder rolling, sliding, and impacting under high-velocity water. Frequency: 10-80 Hz low-frequency irregular impacts. Character: irregular continuous rather than rhythmic. Amplitude: contributes 60-75 dB low-frequency component. Absent at low/moderate flow. THE MOST DISTINCTIVE MAXIMUM-DISCHARGE ACOUSTIC SIGNATURE.
acoustic_data.standing_wave: Hydraulic oscillation at canyon constrictions and below obstacles. Frequency: 0.5-3 Hz (same mechanism as SPS-332 river component analysis). Amplitude at snowmelt maximum: annual peak. Physically felt at canyon proximity (pressure pulsation). Structures the continuous broadband noise into a rhythmic pattern.
acoustic_data.canyon_amplification: Rocky narrow canyon reverb time 1-3 seconds. Direct river sound and multiple canyon reflections overlap. Canyon acts as natural acoustic amplifier. Same river discharge is louder in canyon terrain than in open valley terrain by 10-15 dB depending on canyon geometry.
acoustic_data.e7_calibration: E=7 through sustained enveloping broadband complexity. Sustained 65-80 dB across full frequency spectrum. Physical envelopment quality at canyon proximity: standing wave pulsation felt in body, broadband noise masks all other sounds, spray in air. Bedload transport drumming below the broadband provides the distinctive signature absent at lower flows. Not E=8-9 because no single-impulse peak (no impact concentration of waterfall); E=7 through sustained enveloping complexity rather than maximum impulse.

nodes.title: Bedload Transport Drumming -- Maximum Discharge Acoustic Signature
nodes.description: Rock and boulder rolling at 10-80 Hz during peak snowmelt discharge. Present only at maximum annual flow; absent at low/moderate flow. The most distinctive acoustic signature of the snowmelt flood surge, identifying it specifically as a maximum-discharge event rather than a generic high-flow event.

nodes.title: Salmon Smolt Outmigration -- Concurrent with Peak Flood
nodes.description: Juvenile salmon (smolts) time downstream migration to spring high flows, using the flood pulse as transport corridor from headwater rearing habitat to estuary. The acoustic event at degree 339 coincides with the salmon smolt outmigration -- the sound is concurrent with one of the Pacific Northwest ecosystem's most critical annual biological events.

nodes.title: Declining Snowpack -- Sound of Past State
nodes.description: Pacific Northwest snowpack declining due to warming winters. Flood pulse shifting earlier, compressing, summer flows decreasing. The degree-339 sound is increasingly a document of the past state of Pacific Northwest hydrology. Each decade the traditional May-June peak is smaller and earlier.

cross_references.relationship: E=7 + E=7 = E=14. The band named Green River against the snowmelt flood surge. Both are the spring building moment: proto-grunge builds toward Nirvana E=9 one degree later; the snowmelt river builds toward summer maximum.

The river component of SPS-332 was assessed at E=7-8, consistent with the standalone E=7 assessment here. The same river system in the same spring peak, documented first as part of the polyrhythm (332) and then as a standalone entry (339).
E=7 to E=9. Spring river leads to orca summer behavioral apex. Both are culminating events of their seasonal cycle.
